## Summary

This 2026 guide from Digital Applied provides a comprehensive, opinionated breakdown of how to build topical authority through content clusters in the current post-HCU, post-AI-Overviews landscape. The central argument is that content clusters have displaced individual keyword-optimized posts as the dominant SEO architecture because interconnected, hierarchical content signals genuine expertise rather than surface-level keyword optimization. Topical authority is explicitly distinguished from domain authority: a site can have modest external link counts and still outrank high-DA competitors if it demonstrates deep, coherent topical coverage.

The pillar page specifications are concrete: 3,000-5,000 words for comprehensive coverage, with each subtopic addressed in enough depth to be genuinely useful while leaving room for cluster pages to go deeper. Pages under 2,000 words lack the content density to outrank; pages over 8,000 words risk scope creep that competes with their own cluster pages. Cluster pages target 1,500-2,500 words, going narrow and deep on one specific aspect or question.

For small teams and founders, the cadence advice is practical: "Sites that publish one to two cluster pages per week for a sustained six-month period generate significantly more topical authority than sites that burst-publish 20 pages over two weeks and then go quiet." The guide recommends setting a realistic cadence the team can sustain indefinitely - even two high-quality cluster pages per month compounds meaningfully over 12-18 months.

The internal linking rules are specific: every cluster page must link to the pillar (using keyword-rich anchor text, within the article body where it reads naturally), the pillar must link to every cluster page (within relevant sections, not as a footnote list), and cluster pages should cross-link to 2-3 other cluster pages where there is genuine semantic relationship.

## Key data points / quotes

- Pillar pages: 3,000-5,000 words. Under 2,000 words = insufficient. Over 8,000 words = scope creep risk.
- Cluster pages: 1,500-2,500 words, each covering one specific subtopic.
- Internal linking: every cluster page links to pillar using keyword-rich anchor text; pillar links to all clusters within relevant sections.
- Topical authority is distinct from domain authority: a focused site with 30 aligned backlinks can outrank a generalist site with 300.
- Publishing frequency: "one to two cluster pages per week for a sustained six-month period" generates significantly more topical authority than burst publishing.
- Cadence alternative for small teams: even two high-quality cluster pages per month compounds over 12-18 months.
- "Concentration beats volume" - a site with 25 well-connected articles on a topic can rank better than a generalist outlet with 250 scattered articles.

## Implications for stinger-forge

- Use these word-count ranges as the canonical heuristics in `guides/01-post-length-by-intent.md` for pillar vs. cluster distinction.
- The "1-2 cluster pages per week sustained" vs. "burst publishing" framing is the key insight for `guides/07-cadence-planning.md`.
- The internal linking rules (body placement, keyword-rich anchor text, bidirectional) belong in `guides/00-cluster-pillar-architecture.md` as a checklist.
- The topical authority vs. domain authority distinction is a foundational concept that needs to be explained early in the cluster-pillar guide to help founders prioritize depth over breadth.
